result:
The air was thick with the scent of rain when I finally stepped outside, the kind of rain that hasn’t quite decided whether to fall. I walked without direction, letting the sidewalk cracks guide me like some ancient map etched by forgotten feet. A single leaf, copper and curled like a secret, clung to the toe of my boot—stowaway from a tree I couldn’t name. I didn’t shake it off. Instead, I carried it for blocks, a tiny passenger negotiating the terms of its surrender to gravity.  

Somewhere, a window slammed, and a voice—sharp, maternal—cut through the hush before being swallowed again by bricks. I thought of how we’re all just windows, opening and closing against the weather of each other. My own mother used to stand at ours, forehead pressed to the glass, counting thunderclaps like rosary beads. She believed storms kept score.  

At the corner, a payphone stood severed from its cord, receiver dangling like a hanged man’s shoe. I picked it up, heard nothing—no dial tone, no oceanic static, no ghost breathing my name backward. Just the absence of listening. Still, I whispered “I’m here” into the black mouthpiece, unsure whether I was confessing or announcing arrival.  

The leaf finally let go when I turned homeward, skittering beneath a parked car to continue its life as debris. I didn’t look back. By the time the first real drop hit my cheek, I’d already forgotten what I wanted to say to the rain, though my lips kept moving, rehearsing a shapeless apology for everything that refuses to stay.

message.content:

I walked without direction, letting the sidewalk cracks guide me like some ancient map etched by forgotten feet. A single leaf, copper and curled like a secret, clung to the toe of my boot—stowaway from a tree I couldn’t name. I didn’t shake it off. Instead, I carried it for blocks, a tiny passenger negotiating the terms of its surrender to gravity.  

Somewhere, a window slammed, and a voice—sharp, maternal—cut through the hush before being swallowed again by bricks. I thought of how we’re all just windows, opening and closing against the weather of each other. My own mother used to stand at ours, forehead pressed to the glass, counting thunderclaps like rosary beads. She believed storms kept score.  

At the corner, a payphone stood severed from its cord, receiver dangling like a hanged man’s shoe. I picked it up, heard nothing—no dial tone, no oceanic static, no ghost breathing my name backward. Just the absence of listening. Still, I whispered “I’m here” into the black mouthpiece, unsure whether I was confessing or announcing arrival.  

The leaf finally let go when I turned homeward, skittering beneath a parked car to continue its life as debris. I didn’t look back. prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
